item.title: Iran War Live Updates: Oil and Gas Prices Soar After New Attacks on Gulf Energy Sites
item.description: Refineries and gas facilities in Kuwait, Qatar and Saudi Arabia were struck, a day after Iran’s vast South Pars gas field was hit. The sustained attacks heightened fears over the war’s damage to the global economy.

item.title: Police investigating bailout email from Mandelson to Epstein
item.description: Met chief Sir Mark Rowley says police are looking at whether the email was a criminal offence.

item.title: Ecuador gang leader wanted for murder of presidential candidate arrested
item.description: Officials believe the man - known as Lobo Menor - is linked to the killing of Fernando Villavicencio in 2023.

item.title: British right must not allow prejudice to be masked as principle
item.description: In history, curtailing religious freedom has only ever been the road to horror — the Tories can choose not to repeat it

item.title: The Soldier Who Came Back From the Dead
item.description: Told that Nazar Daletskyi had died, his Ukrainian family buried what they thought were his remains. He turned up three years later in a prisoner-of-war exchange.

item.title: A Lebanese Cafe on the Edge of Destruction
item.description: Near the center of Beirut, one image captures the aftermath of an Israeli strike.

item.title: BoJ holds rates and signals risks to growth and inflation from Iran war
item.description: Limited new information was made available on when the central bank might move next

item.title: Three Palestinian women killed as missile debris hits West Bank beauty salon
item.description: Several other people were injured when shrapnel struck the beauty salon in Beit Awwa, in the southern West Bank.

item.title: How Japan’s Leader Sanae Takaichi Found Her Voice in D.C. Decades Ago
item.description: Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi got her start in politics as an intern for a trailblazing Colorado lawmaker. She returns to Washington this week to meet with President Trump.

item.title: Useful Local Travel Apps to Download Before You Go Abroad
item.description: Ride-hailing, dining and navigation apps you rely on at home may not be the best options in many countries. Here are local alternatives to download before you go.

item.title: Trump Says U.S. and Qatar Not Involved in Strike on Iran’s South Pars Gas Field
item.description: President Trump said Israel was responsible for the attack, but vowed to “massively” destroy the gas field if Tehran hit Qatar’s energy facilities in retaliation.

item.title: The Weather Is Getting Wilder, and Some See a Dire Signal in the Data
item.description: Several of the Earth’s systems are changing faster than predicted as global temperatures rise, scientists say.

item.title: How Immigration Became an Issue in Argentina
item.description: Argentina has often stood out for its openness to immigration. Under President Javier Milei, it has started to crack down.

item.title: Pay grows at slowest rate in more than five years
item.description: Annual earnings grew at an annual rate of 3.8% in the November to January period, the Office for National Statistics says.

item.title: India's ceramic hub grinds to a halt as Iran war chokes gas supply
item.description: Manufacturers stopped operations after supplies of propane and natural gas reduced sharply in recent days.

item.title: Thai parliament backs first conservative PM this century
item.description: Strong majority for Anutin Charnvirakul could bring political stability to south-east Asia’s second-largest economy

item.title: House of Lords backs bid to decriminalise abortion
item.description: Abortion is a crime in England and Wales, unless under strict circumstances, under a 164-year-old law.

item.title: DR Congo and Rwanda agree to ease tensions after talks in US
item.description: The conflict has persisted despite a peace deal signed by DR Congo and Rwanda in December.

item.title: UK unemployment rate held at post-pandemic high of 5.2% at start of the year
item.description: Figure comes ahead of Bank of England decision on interest rates

item.title: Iran inflicts ‘extensive damage’ on site of world’s largest LNG facility in Qatar
item.description: Tehran vowed to retaliate against Gulf energy plants after Israel struck its South Pars gasfield

item.title: Chair of India’s largest private bank quits over ‘ethical differences’
item.description: Central bank calms market saying ‘there are no material concerns’ about the health of HDFC Bank

item.title: Trump says US 'knew nothing' about Israel's attack on major Iran gas field
item.description: Qatar reports "extensive damage" at an energy industry site as Iran retaliates for gas field attack.

item.title: Sadiq Khan says Labour should pledge to rejoin EU
item.description: Downing Street has repeatedly said the government will not rejoin the single market or customs union.

item.title: E.U. Loan for Ukraine Becomes Election Leverage for Orban
item.description: European leaders are pushing Prime Minister Viktor Orban to stop blocking a €90 billion loan to Ukraine. But the issue is a rallying cry in remarkably close elections in Hungary set for April 12.

item.title: Olympic cyclist who ran over wife under fire over Porsche post
item.description: The post was criticised for being "deeply offensive" by a victims' rights commissioner.

item.title: The Fed can only wait and wonder
item.description: Plus, the stock market’s wartime reversal

item.title: Indonesia detains four soldiers over acid attack on activist
item.description: Andrie Yunus, who criticised the army's role in government, suffered burns to 20% of his body.

item.title: The Iran War’s Economic Threat to Europe and Asia
item.description: Inflation and its consequences for growth are a growing concern for countries where memories of the 2022 energy crisis are fresh.

item.title: South Africans say criminal gangs are exploiting the water crisis
item.description: Residents of some areas have been without mains water for weeks.

item.title: Middle East war gatecrashes EU leaders’ economy summit
item.description: Also in this newsletter: DHL Express Europe looks on the bright side

item.title: How the Iran war has left Europe facing yet another energy crisis
item.description: It is not the first time that there has been deep energy-linked frustration in the heart of Europe.

item.title: FirstFT: Oil jumps after Iran strikes Qatari LNG site
item.description: Also in today’s newsletter: Hormuz naval escorts and European private credit

item.title: As a Meningitis Outbreak Spreads in Kent, UK, Here’s What to Know
item.description: At least 20 young adults are believed to be infected, and two have died, in an outbreak of meningococcal disease in the county of Kent, in southeast England.

item.title: Kirkland & Ellis, the gilded age
item.description: The firm becomes first to break $10bn in annual revenues while equity partners take home $11.1mn

item.title: Liberal Netherlands will play ‘active’ role in EU, says new PM
item.description: Head of latest Dutch coalition tells European leaders to ‘stop complaining’ and ‘maybe just do a better job’

item.title: Spain is a role model in weathering Iranian oil shocks
item.description: The country’s speedy rollout of renewables has put a lid on electricity bills

item.title: Iran sets its price to end the war
item.description: Islamic regime is digging in for a protracted conflict as it seeks to ensure the US and Israel will not attack again

item.title: Has Trump finally united Europe?
item.description: This week’s rejection of the US president’s Hormuz request is remarkable. But the EU is still at the mercy of events

item.title: Why bank cards may be heading the way of the cheque book
item.description: The £100 cap on contactless card spending is lifted today — but digital wallets matter more for future growth

item.title: Trump sails into battle with Lloyd’s of London
item.description: The market’s historic strength in marine war insurance is being challenged by the US

item.title: Economists have caught the AI bug
item.description: It’s expanding the scope of research but challenging the profession’s process of weeding out mistakes

item.title: US exploring tying naval escorts in Strait of Hormuz to government insurance
item.description: Vessels seeking protection may be required to take out cover from programme run by Development Finance Corporation

item.title: UK considers FOI clampdown as requests soar
item.description: Moves to tighten system likely to be met with resistance from campaigners

item.title: Calls for probe after ex-Army chief pressed minister to buy drones
item.description: Sir Patrick Sanders, an adviser to German start-up Stark, complained that the UK had no orders with the company

item.title: Watch: Real possum spotted on toy animal shelf at Australian airport
item.description: Passengers discovered a real possum nestled among other native Australian toy animals at Hobart Airport, Tasmania.

item.title: In Swaths of Germany, the Far-Right AfD Is Part of the Fabric
item.description: The Alternative for Germany party, or AfD, is shunned in federal politics but is a regional force. This fall, it could win broader power for the first time.

item.title: King praises 'living bridge' with Nigeria at glitzy banquet
item.description: At the state banquet in Windsor Castle, King Charles praises the UK's partnership with Nigeria.

item.title: Pakistan Pauses Afghanistan Airstrikes for Eid
item.description: At least 143 people were killed in a Pakistani airstrike that hit a drug rehabilitation facility in Kabul on Monday, according to a top U.N. official.

item.title: Analysis: Is government going wobbly on its migration plans?
item.description: Chris Mason asks if the UK government will stick to its big immigration shake-up or water it down.

item.title: As Cuba struggles with power cuts, how is the island holding up?
item.description: The BBC's Will Grant reports from the island days after the entire national electric grid collapsed.

item.title: Afghan evacuees in limbo in Qatar camp accuse US of betrayal
item.description: More than 1,100 Afghans evacuated by the US fear their route to promised resettlement is closed for good.

item.title: How Finnish supermarkets are central to the country's defence
item.description: The chains all have detailed plans to follow in the event of the nation going to war.

item.title: UK sets target to boost steel making and cut imports
item.description: Up to half of steel used in Britain should be made there, the government says, as it announces its steel strategy.

item.title: India's young are more educated than ever. So why are so many jobless?
item.description: India’s educational enrolment has surged but jobs, especially good ones, are not keeping pace, a new report finds.

item.title: Labubu film is official with Paddington director at the helm
item.description: The film will combine live action and computer-generated animation and is in "early development".

item.title: 'One of a kind' Messi hits 900 career goals - numbers behind milestone
item.description: After Lionel Messi scored his 900th career goal on Wednesday, BBC Sport looks at the numbers behind the "insane" achievement.

item.title: The Iran war is causing a global energy crisis - can China withstand it?
item.description: With oil supply disrupted, Beijing's oil reserves and renewable energy push are being put to the test.

item.title: Netanyahu Hopes Strikes on Iran Will Lead to Uprising and Regime Change
item.description: Israeli attacks have targeted the command centers of Iran’s repressive, internal security forces in hopes that Iranians will overthrow their rulers. Some see that as wishful thinking.

item.title: Retired Belgian Diplomat, 93, Faces Trial Over 1961 Killing of Congolese Leader
item.description: A Brussels court ruled that Étienne Davignon must stand trial for the murder of Patrice Lumumba, the first prime minister of the Democratic Republic of Congo.

item.title: The Tropical St. Patrick’s Day That Honors African History
item.description: Montserrat treats the holiday as both a national celebration and a more somber milestone: a commemoration of a failed slave rebellion.

item.title: How Ukraine and Europe got caught in a geopolitical lovers’ tiff
item.description: A testing moment for steadfast allies

item.title: Israel destroys river bridges in southern Lebanon
item.description: The health ministry says that 968 people, including more than 100 children, have been killed since 2 March.

item.title: Cost of online passport application could rise to over £100
item.description: The Home Office says the increase will help it "move to a system that meets its costs through those who use it" and rely less on taxpayers.

item.title: Senegal government alleges corruption over Afcon
item.description: The Senegalese government calls for an "independent international investigation" at the Confederation of African Football after it stripped Senegal of the Africa Cup of Nations title.

item.title: For U.S., Unmet Expectations in Iran Fit a Familiar Pattern in the Region
item.description: Iran’s military retaliation, along with the political defiance of its new leaders, evokes a decades-old pattern of unrealized goals for American interventions in the region.

item.title: He Lost a Leg for Russia. Then, He Says, His Country Betrayed Him.
item.description: Imprisoned for murder, Aleksandr Abbasov-Derskhan sought a new start in life and freedom by signing up to fight in Ukraine. But he says promised benefits proved illusory.

item.title: Is cheap energy the key to China gaining AI supremacy?
item.description: It could be its biggest advantage over the West

item.title: One killed as ski gondola crashes down mountain at Swiss resort
item.description: Dramatic footage showed the gondola flipping over several times in the snow before coming to a halt in Engelberg.

item.title: Forty years after the last one was poached, rhinos are back in the wild in Uganda
item.description: The return of southern white rhinos to the country comes after Ugandan poachers once wiped them out.

item.title: Fly tippers face clearing up own rubbish as punishment
item.description: Fly tippers could face up to 20 hours of community service removing rubbish they have illegally dumped.

item.title: Iran's intelligence minister Esmail Khatib killed in air strike
item.description: Israel said it had killed Khatib, whose death was confirmed by Iranian President Masoud Pezeshkian on Wednesday.

item.title: PM urges Badenoch to sack shadow minister over Muslim comments
item.description: Shadow justice secretary Nick Timothy described a mass Muslim public prayer as an "act of domination and division".

item.title: Russian archaeologist can be sent to Ukraine for trial, Polish judge rules
item.description: Archaeologist Alexander Butyagin is accused of conducting illegal excavations in Crimea - Ukraine's peninsula annexed by Russia.

item.title: 'Very difficult to stop': BBC visits scene of Iran cluster bomb strike on Israel
item.description: An elderly couple was killed after a bomb flew into their apartment in central Israel and exploded.

item.title: Rayner warns immigration reforms risk being 'un-British'
item.description: Most migrant workers will have to wait longer to qualify for permanent residence under the government's proposals.

item.title: Celebrations in Morocco but devastation in Senegal after Afcon decision
item.description: Moroccans feel justice has been done but the Senegalese are angry after being stripped of the title.

item.title: 'The longest VAR check in history' - the fallout from shock Afcon decision
item.description: BBC Sport journalists and pundits look at what happened at the chaotic Afcon final, and what comes next.

item.title: A dirty deal with Cuba would be better than the alternatives
item.description: A prolonged blockade risks creating a humanitarian crisis on America’s doorstep

item.title: PM swerves questions on whether he spoke to Mandelson over Epstein friendship
item.description: Conservative leader Kemi Badenoch repeatedly asks if the prime minister spoke to Peter Mandelson about his friendship with Jeffrey Epstein before picking him to be a UK ambassador.

item.title: Venezuelans celebrate historic World Baseball Classic victory over USA
item.description: Venezuela won its first-ever World Baseball Classic title, defeating the United States 3–2 in the championship game.

item.title: Hittler faces Zielinski as French town's election contest goes viral
item.description: Charles Hittler, mayor of Arcis-sur-Aube, has always faced jokes about his name but now he faces a rival with another unlikely name.

item.title: The Faroe Islands Are Changing Some of Europe’s Strictest Abortion Rules
item.description: A new law allowing abortion up to 12 weeks will be a major shift in an archipelago of 55,000 people, and there are strong feelings on both sides.

item.title: Greens push for billions to be spent on energy support if bills rise
item.description: Leader Zack Polanksi says ministers should end "uncertainty" over future prices caused by the conflict.

item.title: Why the Strait of Hormuz matters so much in the Iran war
item.description: Iran says it will "set fire" to ships trying to sail through the world's most vital oil transit point.

item.title: Government backtracks on AI and copyright after outcry from major artists
item.description: However, the government's position is now unclear, saying it "no longer has a preferred option" for what to do next.

item.title: Argentina make pitch to host 2035 Rugby World Cup
item.description: Argentina is pressing forward with a bid to host the 2035 Rugby World Cup, which would take the event to South America for the first time.

item.title: MSP rules out reintroducing assisted dying bill after Scottish election
item.description: Liam McArthur said it was time to pass the baton to someone else after five years of work on the now-rejected bill.

item.title: Afcon final chaos - key questions answered
item.description: Our Ask Me Anything team look at why the 2025 Afcon final result has been overturned - and what it means.

item.title: Norway prosecutor asks for seven years' jail for crown princess's son in rape trial
item.description: Prosecutors in the trial of Marius Borg Høiby argue he should be found guilty of 39 of the 40 charges he is accused of.

item.title: Middle East Dispatch newsletter: Iran’s mood shifts
item.description: The immediate imperative is no longer political change but personal safety, writes Nick Pelham, our Middle East correspondent

item.title: A spy scandal upends Slovenia’s election campaign
item.description: Embarrassing videos and a mysterious Israeli intelligence firm

item.title: Why did US and Israel attack Iran and how long could the war last?
item.description: Conflict has spread across the Middle East since the US and Israel launched strikes on Iran on 28 February.

item.title: Why ex-Palace winger Olise is now in Ballon d'Or contention at Bayern
item.description: Michael Olise has been in superb form for Bayern Munich this season, has the French winger put himself in contention for the Ballon d'Or?

item.title: Ukraine faces missile shortage due to Middle East war, says Zelensky
item.description: In an exclusive interview with the BBC, the Ukrainian president says Putin wants a "long war" between the US and Iran.

item.title: Chinese national charged for trying to smuggle 2,000 ants from Kenya
item.description: Zhang Kequn was found with the large consignment of ants in his luggage at a Nairobi airport.

item.title: Will the Iran war squeeze India's piped gas next?
item.description: The Iran war has already rattled LPG users. Will India’s piped gas network face the next squeeze?

item.title: How TrumpRx Drug Prices Compare With Those in Other Countries
item.description: The TrumpRx website claims to offer the best prices for medications. Here’s where Americans still pay more — and much more.

item.title: Suspected stray bullet from military drill hits girl at playground
item.description: South Korea's military has since halted all shooting drills involving small firearms.

item.title: India's cheap weight-loss drugs could reshape global obesity fight
item.description: With India’s semaglutide patent expiring on 20 March, about 50 branded generics are expected to enter the market.

item.title: Scotland's assisted dying bill rejected after emotional debate
item.description: Scotland would have become the first part of the UK to legalise the process had MSPs backed the proposals.

item.title: Bodo/Glimt's fairytale run comes to crashing halt
item.description: Bodo/Glimt's dream run in this season's Champions League may be over, but they bow out with their heads held high after making history.

item.title: Watch: Sean Penn receives 'Oscar' in Ukraine after skipping US ceremony
item.description: The Academy Award winning US actor won his third Oscar on Sunday, but skipped the ceremony to visit Ukraine.

item.title: Italy warns Russian tanker could explode in Mediterranean at any time
item.description: Italy is one of nine EU countries urging the European Commission to act over the Arctic Metagaz, drifting out of control in the Mediterranean Sea.

item.title: Spanish king reopens debate on conquest of Mexico by acknowledging 'abuse'
item.description: King Felipe appears to have helped thaw frosty relations with Mexico while reviving a fierce debate over events 500 years ago.

item.title: Air strike hit Kabul rehab centre as patients ate dinner, survivor tells BBC
item.description: The exact number killed in Monday night's strike by Pakistan is still not known, but is feared to be in the hundreds.

item.title: Mayors to gain more spending power under Reeves tax plans
item.description: The chancellor sets out the government's plan for economic growth, which also includes closer ties to the EU.

item.title: Iran and Russia are 'brothers in hatred', Zelensky tells MPs
item.description: The president highlights how Tehran is selling drones to Moscow that have been used against Ukraine.

item.title: Africa’s richest man has ambitious plans for the continent
item.description: But the tycoon also has his critics

item.title: What have US allies and China said about sending ships to the Strait of Hormuz?
item.description: US allies have either pushed back or remained cautious on Trump's initial request to send ships to the crucial oil route.

item.title: Are there enough missile interceptors?
item.description: Shortages in Israel and the Gulf could determine the war’s course

item.title: Water supply cut in Moldova after oil spill blamed on Russian strike in Ukraine
item.description: Moldova has imposed a 15-day state of alert in the Dniester river basin as a precaution.

item.title: Ministers still wrangling over delayed defence investment plan
item.description: MPs ask defence bosses why a vital investment plan promised before Christmas is still on hold.

item.title: Chile's president begins building border barrier less than week into term
item.description: José Antonio Kast visited the ditch - a few feet wide and deep - on Monday, calling it a "milestone".

item.title: Belgian court clears way for trial over 1961 killing of Congo PM Lumumba
item.description: Etienne Davignon, a 93-year-old ex-diplomat, is accused of involvement in the assassination of the independence hero.

item.title: Millions without electricity as Cuba's power grid collapses
item.description: Cuba's chronic fuel shortages have been exacerbated by a US blockade on oil shipments to the island.

item.title: UN receives reports alleging torture of detainees in Venezuela continues
item.description: The UN is deeply concerned by allegations there have been incidents of torture after Nicolás Maduro's ouster.

item.title: Trump 'not happy' with UK response to Iran conflict
item.description: It comes after Sir Keir Starmer said the UK was working with allies on a plan to protect the Strait of Hormuz.

item.title: Arsenal trial and self-doubt: Valverde's journey to Real legend
item.description: Rejected by Arsenal as a teenager, Federico Valverde has transformed himself into a Real Madrid legend - and was the main man in their Champions League win over Manchester City last week.

item.title: Young Venezuelans voice hope and frustration as post-Maduro future unfolds
item.description: Young people who have only known one political system are adjusting to change after US forces seized Maduro.

item.title: Why did only two Iranian football players stay in Australia?
item.description: After Australia gave humanitarian visas to seven team members, only two decided to stay - the BBC's Katy Watson explains what happened.

item.title: Israel says 'limited' ground operations under way in Lebanon
item.description: Troops are targeting "Hezbollah strongholds" in south Lebanon, where strikes reportedly killed nine people on Monday.

item.title: India's outsourcing industry is worth $300bn. Can it survive AI?
item.description: Indian IT stocks have plunged as fears grow of AI disrupting back offices. But some say these are overblown.

item.title: Surge in US gas prices deepens political peril for Trump over Iran
item.description: The odds of a quick resolution to the war appear to be dwindling by the day, and the political stakes for the president are only growing.

item.title: 'Fire came from the sky and burned them' - life on the brink of civil war in South Sudan
item.description: Some 50,000 people are told to flee their homes as the army warns of a huge military offensive in Jonglei state.

item.title: The quiet recovery of Ireland’s ancient tongue
item.description: In the land of St Patrick, Irish is making a steady comeback

item.title: How might the UK support shipping in the Strait of Hormuz?
item.description: Ben Chu from BBC Verify has been looking at what role the Royal Navy could play in the Strait of Hormuz.

item.title: Fifa sanctions Nigeria and DR Congo over World Cup play-off
item.description: Fifa fines the Nigeria Football Federation and DR Congo Football Association for separate disciplinary breaches by supporters.

item.title: 'My mother cried out one last time': Palestinian boy, 12, describes how Israeli forces killed his family in car
item.description: Khaled Bani Odeh's parents and two brothers were shot dead as they drove home from a shopping trip in the occupied West Bank.

item.title: Wary allies show there's no quick fix to Trump's Iran crisis
item.description: European leaders are hesitant to help Trump secure the Strait of Hormuz, but they know inaction on the Iran war is not really an option.

item.title: Will America’s Asian allies get dragged into the Iran war?
item.description: They are worried Uncle Sam will abandon them if they don’t fight

item.title: Mexico City breaks Guinness Record with giant football training session
item.description: Mexico City set a Guinness record for the largest football training session as 9,500 participants filled the capital’s Zocalo square ahead of World Cup.

item.title: The War Room newsletter: A conflict Trump was ill-prepared for
item.description: There is no simple way to reopen the Strait of Hormuz, writes Shashank Joshi, our defence editor

item.title: A Maoist survival guide to the Iranian energy crisis
item.description: For a self-reliant power like China, no supplier is ever irreplaceable

item.title: Ecuador deploys 75,000 soldiers and police to combat drug gangs
item.description: Citizens of the most violent-wracked provinces have been warned the government is "at war" with the gangs.

item.title: Who wants what from the Iran war?
item.description: Most people, although not everybody, want this war to end as quickly as possible. But on what terms?

item.title: Far-left and far-right gains throw French mainstream parties into a quandary
item.description: Awkward choices emerge for mainstream parties after the first round of mayoral elections.
